Title: Biochemistry of high stearic sunflower, a new source of saturated fats

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Low activity toward stearic acid constrains stearate activation and accumulation into sunflower seed oil; mainly supports activation of de novo exported oleate for TAG synthesis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in developing sunflower seeds throughout the studied seed development period.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HaLACS1 was cloned from developing sunflower seeds and is closely related to Arabidopsis AtLACS9/8-type enzymes. 2. It localizes primarily to the plastid envelope, with possible additional ER localization. 3. It shows high activity toward oleic, palmitoleic, linoleic and α-linolenic acids, but low activity toward stearic acid. 4. Its poor activity with stearate is proposed to limit stearic acid accumulation in high stearic sunflower.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Characterized by GFP fusion transient expression in tobacco cells and activity assays in E. coli membranes; possible dual localization remains uncertain.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve stearate-rich oil by engineering LACS variants with higher stearate activation capacity.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats provide healthier hard-stock alternatives to hydrogenated fats and are useful for industrial and nutritional applications.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; phylogenetic analysis; GFP subcellular localization in tobacco cells; heterologous overexpression in E. coli; enzyme substrate specificity assays; expression profiling during seed development.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Low stearate activation may contribute to reduced oil accumulation in high stearic mutants; engineering altered specificity may affect broader acyl-CoA metabolism.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed oil / triacylglycerol in developing seeds
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developing seeds; developmental stage during seed filling
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase family; membrane-associated acyl-activating enzyme
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in developing seeds during the whole period studied.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid envelope; possible additional ER localization

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Low activity toward stearic acid constrains stearate activation in ER-associated acyl turnover and TAG assembly.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Lower expression in seeds, peaking at 25 DAA; also expressed in germinating cotyledons and leaves, with levels comparable to roots and stems.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HaLACS2 was cloned from sunflower seeds and is related to Arabidopsis AtLACS8-type enzymes. 2. It localizes exclusively to the ER. 3. It is most active with palmitic and linoleic acids, lower with oleic and α-linolenic acids, and poor with stearic acid. 4. Its low stearate activity is proposed to constrain stearate accumulation in sunflower oil.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: May function in ER acyl editing/turnover rather than primary plastid export.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible engineering target to improve ER stearoyl-CoA formation and stearate incorporation into TAG.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats provide healthier hard-stock alternatives to hydrogenated fats and are useful for industrial and nutritional applications.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; phylogenetic analysis; GFP subcellular localization in tobacco cells; heterologous overexpression in E. coli; enzyme substrate specificity assays; expression profiling.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing acyl activation specificity could perturb ER phospholipid acyl turnover and seed oil composition.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed oil / triacylglycerol in developing seeds
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developing seeds; developmental stage during seed filling
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase family; membrane-associated acyl-activating enzyme
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Lower in seeds with maximum at 25 DAA; higher in germinating cotyledons and leaves.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch-controlling step converting stearoyl-ACP toward oleoyl-ACP early in fatty acid desaturation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced SAD activity decreases seed oil content, although germination is not affected; may cause broader flux restrictions in TAG biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a double bond at the Δ9 position of stearoyl-ACP to form oleoyl-ACP in plastids.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Plastidial de novo fatty acid synthesis and desaturation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product accumulation by converting the stearate precursor into oleate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic studies of high stearic mutants; mutant phenotyping; fatty acid profiling; correlation analysis with linoleic/oleic backgrounds.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Mutant lines including CAS-3; high oleic background line Pervenets mentioned
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced SAD activity is the primary cause of elevated stearic acid in high stearic sunflower seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for stearate versus oleate partitioning
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High oleic versus high linoleic genetic background affects stearate accumulation; seed developmental context
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A mutation in one sunflower SAD gene accounts for most of the increase in seed stearic acid. 2. Reduced SAD activity is the principal determinant of the high stearic phenotype. 3. High oleic backgrounds tend to reduce stearate accumulation, implying regulation linked to overall unsaturation state. 4. Lower SAD activity also associates with reduced seed oil content.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Soluble plastidial Δ9 acyl-ACP desaturase; mixed-function oxygenase
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A second mutation also enhances stearic acid but its gene is unidentified.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Central target for breeding high stearic sunflower through mutation selection or desaturase suppression, but must be balanced against lower oil content.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal plastid export step of de novo fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced activity increases saturated fatty acids and TAG in leaves; altered flux may reduce polyunsaturated fatty acids and shift membrane-associated fatty acid balance.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed up to 100-fold higher than HaFatB in developing sunflower seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes acyl-ACP thioesters, especially oleoyl-ACP, releasing free fatty acid and ACP for export from plastids.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Acyl-ACP termination/export in plastid fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and can divert flux away from plastid SAD to increase stearate export
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; heterologous expression and purification from E. coli; enzyme kinetics; site-directed mutagenesis; transient expression in tobacco leaves; expression in Arabidopsis seeds; lipid profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Native HaFatA mainly promotes oleate export and its low specificity toward stearate constrains stearic acid accumulation; enhanced alleles increase saturated fatty acid export and TAG accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for plastid fatty acid export composition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in developing sunflower seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developing seeds; transient leaf expression; Arabidopsis seed expression
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HaFatA is the major sunflower acyl-ACP thioesterase expressed in developing seeds. 2. It is chloroplast targeted, soluble, and highly selective for oleoyl-ACP. 3. Its low activity toward stearoyl-ACP limits stearate export from plastids. 4. Manipulating HaFatA activity can increase flux of saturated fatty acids and TAG accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed oil / TAG; experimentally also leaf TAG
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Acyl-ACP thioesterase FatA family; hot-dog domain; chloroplast transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phylogenetically close to Carthamus tinctorius FatA; very high catalytic efficiency versus several species.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Free fatty acid (mainly oleic acid) + ACP
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering enhanced FatA alleles can raise stearate export and storage lipid accumulation, potentially useful in sunflower improvement or vegetative oil engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal plastid export step of de novo fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Low expression limits impact on saturated fatty acid export; increasing FatB may alter palmitate levels alongside stearate.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Much lower than HaFatA in developing sunflower seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes acyl-ACP thioesters with preference for palmitoyl-ACP and activity also toward oleoyl-ACP and lower toward stearoyl-ACP.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Acyl-ACP termination/export in plastid fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for saturated fatty acids but limited contribution to stearate accumulation due to low expression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli under low temperature/IPTG; enzyme substrate specificity characterization; expression comparison.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to export of saturated fatty acids, but low expression relative to HaFatA limits its role in stearic acid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; contributory constraint due to low expression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed at much lower levels than HaFatA in developing seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HaFatB is a chloroplast-targeted acyl-ACP thioesterase with membrane anchorage. 2. It prefers palmitoyl-ACP, with activity also toward oleoyl-ACP and less toward stearoyl-ACP. 3. Its expression is far lower than HaFatA in developing seeds. 4. Low HaFatB abundance is considered another constraint on stearate accumulation in sunflower oil.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Acyl-ACP thioesterase FatB family; hot-dog domain; N-terminal membrane anchor; chloroplast transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Purification was difficult due to inclusion bodies and toxicity upon strong bacterial expression.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Free fatty acid (mainly palmitic acid) + ACP
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be manipulated to increase saturated fatty acid export, but effects on palmitate/stearate balance require caution.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Engineered terminal plastid export step of fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increases saturated fatty acids in TAG and leaf TAG accumulation; may alter normal fatty acid balance though oil content in Arabidopsis seeds was unaffected.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered allele; expression pattern not separately reported from native gene.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Engineered HaFatA variant with higher catalytic efficiency for acyl-ACP hydrolysis without major change in substrate selectivity.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Acyl-ACP termination/export in plastid fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Oleoyl-ACP; retains low but functional activity toward stearoyl-ACP
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and diverts carbon away from plastid desaturation to increase stearate export
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed mutagenesis; enzyme kinetics; transient expression in tobacco leaves; transgenic expression in Arabidopsis seeds; TAG and fatty acid profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improved HaFatA allele raises TAG accumulation and increases stearate and palmitate in transgenic tissues, demonstrating enhanced export of saturated fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Engineered enhancement of a key export control step
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient expression in tobacco leaves; seed expression in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Q215W mutation increased catalytic efficiency about 2.4-fold over wild-type HaFatA. 2. Substrate selectivity profile remained broadly unchanged. 3. Expression in tobacco leaves doubled to quadrupled TAG accumulation and increased palmitate and stearate in TAG. 4. Expression in Arabidopsis seeds also increased saturated fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf TAG in tobacco; seed oil in Arabidopsis; proposed seed oil in sunflower
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Developed by substituting active-site residue with bulky hydrophobic tryptophan.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising engineering allele to enhance plastidial export of stearate and increase saturated TAG accumulation in crop improvement.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: First acylation step of the Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong substrate inhibition by stearoyl-CoA may reduce overall flux into TAGs in high stearic lines and lower seed oil content.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: The review states sunflower GPAT1 seemed to be the isoform mainly involved in TAG biosynthesis; detailed expression not reported.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acyl group from acyl-CoA to the sn-1 position of glycerol-3-phosphate to form lysophosphatidic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: ER Kennedy pathway for TAG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Glycerol-3-phosphate plus linoleoyl-CoA/palmitoyl-CoA preferred; very low activity with stearoyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage incorporation of stearate into TAG, but competes against target accumulation because it poorly uses stearoyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microsomal enzyme assays in developing sunflower seeds; gene cloning of sunflower ER GPATs; substrate specificity analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Poor utilization of stearoyl-CoA and strong inhibition by saturated acyl-CoAs restrict stearate incorporation at sn-1 and limit high stearic oil accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Bottleneck step for stearate incorporation into TAG
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: ER GPAT genes are expressed in developing seeds; GPAT1 suggested as main TAG-biosynthetic isoform.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developing seeds; high stearoyl-CoA pool in high stearic lines
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sunflower microsomal GPAT prefers linoleoyl-CoA and palmitoyl-CoA, shows lower activity with oleoyl-CoA, and hardly detectable activity with stearoyl-CoA. 2. Acyl-CoAs strongly inhibit the enzyme at higher concentrations. 3. GPAT selectivity contributes to low incorporation of stearate at sn-1 and asymmetric TAG structure. 4. An exogenous GPAT with high stearoyl-CoA specificity is proposed as a better engineering solution than merely overexpressing sunflower GPAT1.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Two sunflower ER GPATs were cloned, but individual gene-specific biochemical assignments remained under investigation in the review.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major target for engineering stearate-rich sunflower; exogenous stearoyl-CoA-preferring GPAT is recommended to overcome native bottleneck.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Second acylation step of the Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excludes saturated fatty acids from sn-2, limiting trisaturated TAG formation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Two sunflower LPAT genes are ubiquitously expressed; in seeds expression is high early in development and declines sharply after 15 DAA.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acyl group from acyl-CoA to lysophosphatidic acid at the sn-2 position to form phosphatidic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: ER Kennedy pathway for TAG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Oleoyl-CoA preferred; some activity with palmitoyl-CoA and stearoyl-CoA but strongly inhibited
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for TAG assembly but restricts target accumulation by excluding saturated fatty acids from sn-2
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microsomal enzyme characterization; sequence analysis; gene cloning; expression profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: LPAT substrate selectivity largely prevents incorporation of saturated fatty acids, including stearate, at the sn-2 position of sunflower TAGs.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key positional constraint on stearate incorporation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Ubiquitous; in seeds highest at early development then declines after 15 DAA.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sunflower LPAT strongly favors oleoyl-CoA over saturated acyl-CoAs. 2. Saturated fatty acids are largely excluded from the sn-2 position of TAGs even in high stearic seeds. 3. Two sunflower LPAT genes similar to AtLPAT2 were identified and expressed in developing seeds. 4. LPAT selectivity explains the predominance of SUU/SUS over SSS TAG species.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: ER membrane LPAT with four transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Position-specific exclusion of saturated fatty acids is a common feature of vegetable oils, retained in sunflower high stearic mutants.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A promising engineering target if altered specificity LPATs are sought to place stearate at sn-2, though this review emphasizes GPAT as the more urgent bottleneck.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Final acylation step of the Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: DGAT2 shows the highest expression among the three identified sunflower DGAT genes in developing seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acyl group from acyl-CoA to diacylglycerol to form triacylglycerol.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Final TAG assembly in the ER
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Diacylglycerol plus acyl-CoA; specific stearoyl-CoA preference not directly resolved for the individual isoform
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms storage TAG containing stearate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification and sequencing; expression profiling in developing seeds; microsomal DGAT activity assays in sunflower seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Sunflower DGAT activity is relatively permissive for stearoyl-CoA incorporation into TAG, and DGAT2 is the most highly expressed DGAT isoform in developing seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal incorporation step; less restrictive for stearate than GPAT or LPAT
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest expressed DGAT isoform in developing sunflower seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sunflower contains three DGAT genes, including one DGAT2. 2. DGAT2 is unexpectedly the highest expressed isoform in developing seeds. 3. Sunflower microsomes can incorporate stearoyl-CoA into TAGs at significant rates. 4. DGAT activity likely accounts for most stearate entering sunflower TAGs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed triacylglycerol, especially sn-3/final acylation product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Substrate specificity of individual sunflower DGAT isoforms had not yet been directly investigated in this review.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Likely useful for stacking with stearate-export and stearoyl-CoA-incorporating enzymes to improve high stearic oil traits.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Final acylation step of the Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Two sunflower DGAT1 genes are expressed in developing seeds, but below DGAT2.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acyl group from acyl-CoA to diacylglycerol to form triacylglycerol.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Final TAG assembly in the ER
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms storage TAG containing stearate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification and sequencing; expression analysis; prior microsomal DGAT activity characterization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Sunflower DGAT1 contributes to TAG assembly and, at the family level, DGAT activity is one of the less restrictive routes for stearate incorporation into TAGs.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal step; not established as limiting in this review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Two type-1 DGAT genes were identified in sunflower. 2. All DGAT genes are expressed in developing seeds. 3. DGAT activity can incorporate stearoyl-CoA into TAGs at significant rates. 4. Compared with GPAT and LPAT, DGAT represents a less severe constraint on stearate accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stearic-rich fats are useful as healthier saturated fats and industrial hard stocks.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DGAT1 membrane acyltransferase with multiple transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review treats sunflower DGAT family functionally; isoform-specific stearate selectivity was not resolved.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential stacking target with upstream genes to improve final stearate deposition into TAG.
